skill.name: environment-setup
skill.description: Configure and manage development, staging, and production environments. Use when setting up environment variables, managing configurations, or separating environments. Handles .env files, config management, and environment-specific settings.
skill.summary: Set up environment variables and manage configuration across development, staging, and production environments
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation/education resource that provides templates and best practices for environment configuration. Static analysis flagged many patterns (credential examples, dotenv usage, environment variable access) but these are all placeholder examples in documentation, not actual executable code. The skill declares Read Write Edit Bash permissions, which is appropriate for configuration management. No malicious intent detected - all flagged patterns are legitimate documentation content. Users should be aware this skill can modify configuration files.

medium_findings.title: Educational Documentation Contains Credential Patterns
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md contains example .env templates with placeholder credentials (DATABASE_URL, AWS keys, SMTP passwords). These are documentation examples showing users how to structure their own configuration, not actual secrets. The skill does not exfiltrate or misuse these values.

medium_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions Declared
medium_findings.description: The skill declares allowed-tools: Read Write Edit Bash, providing broad file system and command execution capabilities. This is necessary for configuration management but means the skill could modify any file if given malicious instructions.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access in Documentation
low_findings.description: Code examples show process.env usage patterns. These are educational examples teaching users how to access environment variables in TypeScript applications, not actual runtime environment access by the skill.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Documentation contains example URLs (localhost:3000, sentry.io, myapp.com). These are placeholder examples showing URL formats, not actual network endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Heuristic: Documentation Pattern Confusion
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis flagged this as a dangerous combination (code execution + network + credential access). Upon review, these patterns appear in example code blocks within markdown documentation, not actual executable code. The skill is teaching configuration management patterns, not performing these operations.

content.user_title: Set up environment variables across dev, staging, and production
content.value_statement: Managing environment variables across multiple environments is error-prone and time-consuming. This skill provides templates and best practices for .env files, TypeScript configuration validation, and Docker environment handling so you can set up environments correctly the first time.
actual_capabilities: Generate .env.example templates with required environment variables
Create TypeScript configuration with Zod validation for type-safe environment access
Set up per-environment config files for development, staging, and production
Configure Docker environment variables with docker-compose
Implement .gitignore rules to prevent committing secrets
Handle environment variable interpolation for production deployments
limitations: Does not execute shell commands or scripts
Cannot connect to databases or external services to validate credentials
Requires manual security review of generated configurations before deployment

use_cases.title: Initialize environment configuration for a new project
use_cases.description: Set up a complete environment configuration structure including .env.example, TypeScript validation, and per-environment configs to ensure consistent setup across your team.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developers starting a new project

use_cases.title: Migrate legacy configuration to type-safe environment handling
use_cases.description: Replace ad-hoc process.env access with validated Zod schemas and centralized configuration management for improved reliability and type safety.

use_cases.title: Prepare production deployment configuration
use_cases.description: Create production-specific environment templates with variable interpolation for secrets injection from CI/CD pipelines or secret managers.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers and site reliability engineers

prompt_templates.title: Create .env template for my Node.js project
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a .env.example template for a Node.js application that uses PostgreSQL, Redis, and sends email via SMTP. Include common variables for authentication, database pooling, and feature flags.

prompt_templates.title: Add environment validation to existing config
prompt_templates.prompt: Add Zod validation to our existing config/index.ts to ensure all required environment variables are present and properly formatted at startup. Include error handling with clear messages.

prompt_templates.title: Set up multi-environment Docker configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a docker-compose.yml with separate environment configurations for development and production. Use env_file for local development and environment variables for production. Include PostgreSQL and Redis services.

prompt_templates.title: Implement secrets rotation preparation
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up environment configuration that supports AWS Secrets Manager for credential injection in production while using local .env files in development. Include validation for both scenarios.

output_examples.input: Create .env template for a Node.js API
output_examples.output: Generated .env.example with sections for Application, Database, Redis, Authentication, Email (SMTP), External APIs (Stripe), AWS, Monitoring (Sentry), and Feature Flags. Each section includes comments explaining the variable purpose.

Created config/env.ts with Zod schema that validates DATABASE_URL is a valid URL, JWT secrets are at least 32 characters, SMTP_PORT is a number, and LOG_LEVEL is one of error/warn/info/debug. Throws descriptive errors on validation failure.
Added rules to prevent committing .env files: .env, .env.local, .env.*.local, and .env.production. Created .gitignore content block ready to copy into project.

best_practices: Always use .env.example as a committed template showing required variables without actual secrets
Implement runtime validation with Zod at application startup to catch missing configuration early
Use environment-specific config files to keep environment-specific logic organized and maintainable
anti_patterns: Do not commit actual .env files with real credentials to version control
Avoid accessing environment variables directly throughout code; use a centralized config module
Do not use weak default values for secrets; require explicit configuration in production

faq.question: How do I keep my secrets safe when using this skill?
faq.answer: Never commit .env files with real credentials. Use .env.example as a template showing required variables, then copy it to .env.local for local development. In production, inject secrets from environment variables, AWS Secrets Manager, or HashiCorp Vault.

faq.question: Can this skill connect to my database to validate credentials?
faq.answer: No, this skill generates configuration templates and code examples. It cannot validate that your database credentials actually work. Test your configurations manually after generation.

faq.question: What is the difference between .env and .env.local?
faq.answer: .env files are typically committed as templates (without secrets) to show team members what variables are needed. .env.local files contain local-only overrides with actual secrets and should be in .gitignore.

faq.question: How do I handle different values for development vs production?
faq.answer: Use separate config files like config/environments/development.ts and config/environments/production.ts. Load the appropriate one based on NODE_ENV. In docker-compose, use environment: for production values and env_file: for local development.

faq.question: Why should I use Zod for environment validation?
faq.answer: Zod validates environment variables at runtime with clear error messages. This catches misconfiguration early before it causes cryptic errors deep in your application logic. It also provides TypeScript type inference.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with languages other than TypeScript?
faq.answer: The skill focuses on TypeScript examples but the core concepts apply to any language. The .env file templates, docker-compose configurations, and .gitignore rules work universally. You would adapt the validation patterns to your language of choice.
